$$\begin{aligned} &\therefore \quad \frac{A+A^{\prime}}{2}+\frac{A-A^{\prime}}{2}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 2 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 2 & -1 & \frac{3}{2} \\ \frac{5}{2} & \frac{3}{2} & 2 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 1 & \frac{-3}{2} \\ -1 & 0 & \frac{1}{2} \\ \frac{3}{2} & \frac{-1}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]\\ &\text { which is the required expression. } \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The matrix $P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a
en.explanation: We know that, in a square matrix number of rows are equal to the number of columns, so the matrix $P=\left[\begin{array}{lll}0 & 0 & 4 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 4 & 0 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a square matrix.

en.content: Total number of possible matrices of order $3\times3$ with each entry 2 or 0 is
en.explanation: Total number of possible matrices of order $3\times3$ with each entry 2 or 0 is $2^9$ i.e., 512.

en.content: $\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 x+y & 4 x \\ 5 x-7 & 4 x\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}7 & 7 y-13 \\ y & x+6\end{array}\right]$, then the value of $x+y$ is
en.explanation:
We have, $$4 x=x+6 \Rightarrow x=2$$

and $4 x=7 y-13 \Rightarrow 8=7 y-13$

$\Rightarrow 7 y=21 \Rightarrow y=3$

$\therefore \quad x+y=2+3=5$

en.content: If $A=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc}\sin ^{-1}(x \pi) & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right) \\ \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right) & \cot ^{-1}(\pi x)\end{array}\right]$ and $B=\frac{1}{\pi}\left[\begin{array}{cc}-\cos ^{-1}(x \pi) & \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right) \\ \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{\pi}\right) & -\tan ^{-1}(\pi x)\end{array}\right]$, then $A-B$ is equal to
en.explanation:
We have, $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}\frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} x \pi & \frac{1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi} \\ \frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi} & \frac{1}{\pi} \cot ^{-1} \pi x\end{array}\right]$

and $$B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} \frac{-1}{\pi} \cos ^{-1} x \pi & \frac{1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi} \\ \frac{1}{\pi} \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi} & \frac{-1}{\pi} \tan ^{-1} \pi x \end{array}\right]$$

$$\begin{array}{rlr} \therefore \quad A-B & =\left[\begin{array}{ll} \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\sin ^{-1} x \pi+\cos ^{-1} x \pi\right) & \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}-\tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}\right) \\ \frac{1}{\pi}\left(\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}-\sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{\pi}\right) & \frac{1}{\pi} \cot ^{-1} \pi x+\tan ^{-1} \pi x \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{lll} \frac{1}{\pi} & \frac{\pi}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & \frac{1}{\pi} & \cdot \frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right] & {\left[\begin{array}{ll} \because \sin ^{-1} x+\cos ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2} \\ \text { and } \tan ^{-1} x+\cot ^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2} \end{array}\right]} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2} I \end{array}$$

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are two matrices of the order $3 \times \mathrm{m}$ and $3 \times n$, respectively and $m=n$, then order of matrix $(5 A-2 B)$ is
en.explanation: $A_{3 \times m}$ and $B_{3 \times n}$ are two matrices. If $m=n$, then $A$ and $B$ have same orders as $3 \times n$ each, so the order of $(5 A-2 B)$ should be same as $3 \times n$.

en.content: If $A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]$, then $A^2$ is equal to
en.explanation: $\because A^2=A \cdot A=\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll}0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]$

en.content: If matrix $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2}$, where $a_{i j}=1$, if $i \neq j=0$ and if $i=j$, then $A^2$ is equal to
en.explanation: $$\begin{aligned} &\text {We have, } A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{2 \times 2} \text {, where } \mathrm{a}_{i j}=1 \text {, if } i \neq j=0 \text { and if } i=j\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \text { and } & A^2=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=I \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

en.content: The matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4\end{array}\right]$ is a
en.explanation:
Let

$$\begin{aligned} & A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore\quad & A^{\prime}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]=A \end{aligned}$$

So, the given matrix is a symmetric matrix. [since, in a square matrix $A$, if $A^{\prime}=A$, then $A$ is called symmetric matrix]

en.content: The matrix $\left[\begin{array}{ccc}0 & -5 & 8 \\ 5 & 0 & 12 \\ -8 & -12 & 0\end{array}\right]$ is a
en.explanation:
We know that, in a square matrix, if $b_{i j}=0$, when $i \neq j$, then it is said to be a diagonal matrix. Here, $b_{12}, b_{13}, \ldots \neq 0$, so the given matrix is not a diagonal matrix.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Now,}\quad B & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -5 & 8 \\ 5 & 0 & 12 \\ -8 & -12 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore\quad B^{\prime} & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & 5 & -8 \\ -5 & 0 & -12 \\ 8 & 12 & 0 \end{array}\right]=-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -5 & 8 \\ 5 & 0 & 12 \\ -8 & -12 & 0 \end{array}\right]=-B \end{aligned}$$

So, the given matrix is a skew-symmetric matrix, since we know that in a square matrix $B$, if $B^{\prime}=-B$, then it is called skew-symmetric matrix.

en.content: If $A$ is matrix of order $m \times n$ and $B$ is a matrix such that $A B^{\prime}$ and $B^{\prime} A$ are both defined, then order of matrix $B$ is
en.explanation:
Let $$A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{m \times n} \text { and } B=\left[b_{i j}\right]_{p \times q}$$

$$\therefore \quad B^{\prime}=\left[b_{j i}\right]_{q \times p}$$

Now, $A B^{\prime}$ is defined, so $n=q$ and $B A$ is also defined, so $p=m$

$\therefore \quad$ Order of $B^{\prime}=\left[b_{j i}\right]_{n \times m}$

and order of $B=\left[b_{i j}\right]_{m \times n}$

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are matrices of same order, then $\left(A B^{\prime}-B A^{\prime}\right)$ is
en.explanation:
We have matrices $A$ and $B$ of same order.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Let}\quad P & =\left(A B^{\prime}-B A^{\prime}\right) \\ \text{Then,}\quad P^{\prime} & =\left(A B^{\prime}-B A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}=\left(A B^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}-\left(B A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} \\ & =\left(B^{\prime}\right)^{\prime}(A)^{\prime}-\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} B^{\prime}=B A^{\prime}-A B^{\prime} \\ & =-\left(A B^{\prime}-B A\right)=-P \end{aligned}$$

Hence, $\left(A B^{\prime}-B A^{\prime}\right)$ is a skew-symmetric matrix.

en.content: If $A$ is a square matrix such that $A^2=I$, then $(A-I)^3+(A+I)^3-7 A$ is equal to
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} &\text { We have, } A^2=I\\ &\begin{aligned} \therefore(A-I)^3+(A+I)^3-7 A= & {\left[(A-I)+(A+I)\left\{(A-I)^2\right.\right.} \\ & \left.\left.+(A+I)^2-(A-I)(A+I)\right\}\right]-7 A \\ & {\left[\because a^3+b^3=(a+b)\left(a^2+b^2-a b\right)\right] } \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{lr} =\left[(2 A)\left\{A^2+I^2-2 A I+A^2+I^2+A I-\left(A^2-I^2\right)\right\}\right]-7 A & \\ =2 A\left[I+I^2+I+I^2-A^2+I^2\right]-7 A & {\left[\because A^2=A I\right]} \\ =2 A[5 I-I]-7 A & \\ =8 A I-7 A I & {[\because A=A I]} \\ =A I=A & \end{array}$$

en.content: For any two matrices $A$ and $B$, we have
en.explanation: For any two matrices $A$ and $B$, we may have $A B=B A=I, A B \neq B A$ and $A B=O$ but it is not always true.

en.content: Q. 66 On using elementary column operations $C_2 \rightarrow C_2-2 C_1$ in the following matrix equation $\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -3 \\ 2 & 4\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4\end{array}\right]$, we have
en.explanation:
Given that, $\left[\begin{array}{lr}1 & -3 \\ 2 & 4\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}3 & 1 \\ 2 & 4\end{array}\right]$

On using $C_2 \rightarrow C_2-2 C_1 \cdot\left[\begin{array}{rr}1 & -5 \\ 2 & 0\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc}1 & -1 \\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}3 & -5 \\ 2 & 0\end{array}\right]$

Since, on using elementary column operation on $X=A B$, we apply these operations simultaneously on $X$ and on the second matrix $B$ of the product $A B$ on RHS.

en.content: On using elementary row operation $R_1 \rightarrow R_1-3 R_2$ in the following matrix equation $\left[\begin{array}{ll}4 & 2 \\ 3 & 3\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$, we have
en.explanation:
We have, $\left[\begin{array}{ll}4 & 2 \\ 3 & 3\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}1 & 2 \\ 0 & 3\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1\end{array}\right]$

Using elementary row operation $R_1 \rightarrow R_1-3 R_2$,

$$\left[\begin{array}{cc} -5 & -7 \\ 3 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & -7 \\ 0 & 3 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{array}\right]$$

Since, on using elementary row operation on $X=A B$, we apply these operation simultaneously on $X$ and on the first matrix $A$ of the product $A B$ on RHS.

en.content: ........... matrix is both symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.
en.explanation: Null matrix is both symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix.

en.content: Sum of two skew-symmetric matrices is always ........... matrix.
en.explanation: Let $A$ is a given matrix, then $(-A)$ is a skew-symmetric matrix. Similarly, for a given matrix $-B$ is a skew-symmetric matrix. Hence, $-A-B=-(A+B) \Rightarrow$ sum of two skew-symmetric matrices is always skew-symmetric matrix.

en.content: The negative of a matrix is obtained by multiplying it by ............ .
en.explanation:
Let $A$ is a given matrix.

$\therefore \quad-A=-1[A]$

So, the negative of a matrix is obtained by multiplying it by $-1$.

en.content: The product of any matrix by the scalar ............. is the null matrix.
en.explanation: The product of any matrix by the scalar 0 is the null matrix. i.e., $0 \cdot A=0$. [where, $A$ is any matrix]

en.content: A matrix which is not a square matrix is called a ........... matrix.
en.explanation: A matrix which is not a square matrix is called a rectangular matrix. For example a rectangular matrix is $A=\left[a_{i j}\right]_{m \times n}$, where $m \neq n$.

en.content: Matrix multiplication is ........... over addition.
en.explanation:
Matrix multiplication is distributive over addition.

e.g., For three matrices $A, B$ and $C$,

(i) $A(B+C)=A B+A C$

(ii) $(A+B) C=A C+B C$

en.content: If $A$ is a symmetric matrix, then $A^3$ is a ............ matrix.
en.explanation:
If $A$ is a symmetric matrix, then $A^3$ is a symmetric matrix.

$$\begin{aligned} \because\quad A^{\prime} & =A \\ \therefore\quad \left(A^3\right)^{\prime} & =A^3 \\ & =A^3\quad \left[\because\left(A^{\prime}\right)^n=\left(A^n\right)^{\prime}\right] \end{aligned}$$

en.content: If $A$ is a skew-symmetric matrix, then $A^2$ is a .............. .
en.explanation:
If $A$ is a skew-symmetric matrix, then $A^2$ is a symmetric matrix.

$$\begin{aligned} \because\quad A^{\prime} & =-A \\ \therefore\quad \left(A^2\right)^{\prime} & =\left(A^{\prime}\right)^2 \\ & =(-A)^2 \\ & =A^2 \end{aligned} \quad\left[\because A^{\prime}=-A\right]$$

So, A$^2$ is a symmetric matrix.

en.content:
If $A$ and $B$ are square matrices of the same order, then

(i) $(A B)^{\prime}=$ ...........

(ii) $(k A)^{\prime}=$ ........... (where, $k$ is any scalar)

(iii) $[k(A-B)]^{\prime}=$ ..............
en.explanation:
(i) $(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}$

(ii) $(k A)^{\prime}=k A^{\prime}$

(iii) $[k(A-B)]^{\prime}=k\left(A^{\prime}-B^{\prime}\right)$

en.content: If $A$ is a skew-symmetric, then $k A$ is a ............ (where, $k$ is any scalar).
en.explanation:
If $A$ is a skew-symmetric, then $k A$ is a skew-symmetric matrix (where, $k$ is any scalar).

$$\left[\because A^{\prime}=-A \Rightarrow(k A)^{\prime}=k(A)^{\prime}=-(k A)\right]$$

en.content:
If $A$ and $B$ are symmetric matrices, then

(i) $A B-B A$ is a .............

(ii) $B A-2 A B$ is a ...........
en.explanation:
(i) $A B-B A$ is a skew-symmetric matrix.

Since,

$$\begin{array}{rlr} {[A B-B A]^{\prime}} & =(A B)^{\prime}-(B A)^{\prime} & \\ & =B^{\prime} A^{\prime}-A^{\prime} B^{\prime} & {\left[\because(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}\right]} \\ & =B A-A B & {\left[\because A^{\prime}=A \text { and } B^{\prime}=B\right]} \\ & =-[A B-B A] & \end{array}$$

So, $[A B-B A]$ is a skew-symmetric matrix.

(ii) $[B A-2 A B]$ is a neither symmetric nor skew-symmetric matrix.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad(B A-2 A B)^{\prime} & =(B A)^{\prime}-2(A B)^{\prime} \\ & =A^{\prime} B^{\prime}-2 B^{\prime} A^{\prime} \\ & =A B-2 B A \\ & =-(2 B A-A B) \end{aligned}$$

So, $[B A-2 A B]$ is neither symmetric nor skew-symmetric matrix.

en.content: If $A$ is symmetric matrix, then $B^{\prime} A B$ is ............ .
en.explanation:
If $A$ is a symmetric matrix, then $B^{\prime} A B$ is a symmetric metrix.

$$\begin{aligned} \because\quad{\left[B^{\prime} A B\right]^{\prime} } & =\left[B^{\prime}(A B)\right]^{\prime} \\ & =(A B)^{\prime}\left(B^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} \quad \left[\because(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}\right]\\ & =B^{\prime} A^{\prime} B\\ & =\left[B^{\prime} A^{\prime} B\right] \quad\left[\because A^{\prime}=A\right] \end{aligned}$$

So, $B^{\prime} A B$ is a symmetric matrix.

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are symmetric matrices of same order, then $A B$ is symmetric if and only if ............. .
en.explanation:
If $A$ and $B$ are symmetric matrices of same order, then $A B$ is symmetric if and only if $A B=B A$.

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore\quad & (A B)^{\prime} \\ = & B^{\prime} A^{\prime}=B A \\ = & A B \end{aligned} \quad[\because A B=B A]$$

en.content: In applying one or more row operations while finding $A^{-1}$ by elementary row operations, we obtain all zeroes in one or more, then $A^{-1} \ldots \ldots .$.
en.explanation: In applying one or more row operations while finding $A^{-1}$ by elementary row operations, we obtain all zeroes in one or more, then $A^{-1}$ does not exist.

en.content: A matrix denotes a number.
en.explanation: A matrix is an ordered rectangular array of numbers or functions.

en.content: Matrices of any order can be added.
en.explanation: Two matrices are added, if they are of the same order.

en.content: Two matrices are equal, if they have same number of rows and same number of columns.
en.explanation: If two matrices have same number of rows and same number of columns, then they are said to be square matrix and if two square matrices have same elements in both the matrices, only then they are called equal.

en.content: Matrices of different order cannot be subtracted.
en.explanation: Two matrices of same order can be subtracted

en.content: Matrix addition is associative as well as commutative.
en.explanation: Matrix addition is associative as well as commutative i.e., $(A+B)+C=A+(B+C)$ and $A+B=B+A$, where $A, B$ and $C$ are matrices of same order.

en.content: Matrix multiplication is commutative.
en.explanation: Since, $A B \neq B A$ is possible when $A B$ and $B A$ are both defined.

en.content: A square matrix where every element is unity is called an identity matrix.
en.explanation: Since, in an identity matrix, the diagonal elements are all one and rest are all zero.

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are two square matrices of the same order, then $A+B=B+A$.
en.explanation: Since, matrix addition is commutative i.e., $A+B=B+A$, where $A$ and $B$ are two square matrices.

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are two matrices of the same order, then $A-B=B-A$.
en.explanation:
Since, the addition of two matrices of same order are commutative.

$$\therefore \quad A+(-B)=A-B=-[B-A] \neq B-A$$

en.content: If matrix $A B=0$, then $A=0$ or $B=0$ or both $A$ and $B$ are null matrices.
en.explanation: Since, for two non-zero matrices $A$ and $B$ of same order, it can be possible that $A \cdot B=0=$ null matrix

en.content: Transpose of a column matrix is a column matrix.
en.explanation: Transpose of a column matrix is a row matrix.

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are two square matrices of the same order, then $A B=B A$.
en.explanation: For two square matrices of same order it is not always true that $A B=B A$.

en.content: If each of the three matrices of the same order are symmetric, then their sum is a symmetric matrix.
en.explanation:
Let $A, B$ and $C$ are three matrices of same order

$$\begin{array}{rlrl} \therefore & A^{\prime} =A, B^{\prime}=B \text { and } C^{\prime}=C \\ \therefore & (A+B+C)^{\prime} =A^{\prime}+B^{\prime}+C^{\prime} \\ & =(A+B+C) \end{array}$$

en.content: If $A$ and $B$ are any two matrices of the same order, then $(A B)^{\prime}=A^{\prime} B^{\prime}$.
en.explanation: $$\because(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}$$

en.content: Q. 96 If $(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}$, where $A$ and $B$ are not square matrices, then number of rows in $A$ is equal to number of columns in $B$ and number of columns in $A$ is equal to number of rows in $B$.
en.explanation:
Let $A$ is of order $m \times n$ and $B$ is of order $p \times q$.

Since, $(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}\quad\text{.... (i)}$

$$\therefore \quad A_{(m \times n)} B(p \times q) \text { is defined } \Rightarrow n=p$$

and $A B$ is of order $m \times q$.

$\Rightarrow(A B)^{\prime}$ is of order $q \times m\quad\text{.... (ii)}$

$$\begin{aligned} &\text { Also, } B^{\prime} \text { is of order } q \times p \text { and } A^{\prime} \text { is of order } n \times m\\ &\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & B^{\prime} A^{\prime} \text { is defined } \Rightarrow p=n \\ \text { and } & B^{\prime} A^{\prime} \text { is of order } q \times m.\quad\text{.... (iii)} \end{array} \end{aligned}$$

Also, equality of matrices $(A B)^{\prime}=B^{\prime} A^{\prime}$, we get the given statement as true.

e.g., If $A$ is of order $(3 \times 1)$ and $B$ is of order $(1 \times 3)$,

we get Order of $(A B)^{\prime}=\operatorname{Order}$ of $\left(B^{\prime} A^{\prime}\right)=3 \times 3$

en.content: If $A, B$ and $C$ are square matrices of same order, then $A B=A C$ always implies that $B=C$.
en.explanation:
If $A B=A C=0$, then it can be possible that $B$ and $C$ are two non-zero matrices such that $B \neq C$.

$$ \begin{array}{ll} \therefore & A \cdot B=0=A \cdot C \\ \text { Let } & A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right] \\ \text { and } & C=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore & A B=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \text { and } & A C=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \cdot\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & A B=A C \quad \text { but } B \neq C \end{array}$$

en.content: $A A^{\prime}$ is always a symmetric matrix for any matrix $A$.
en.explanation: $$\because\left[A A^{\prime}\right]^{\prime}=\left(A^{\prime}\right)^{\prime} A^{\prime}=\left[A A^{\prime}\right]$$

en.content: If $A=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 3 & -1 \\ 1 & 4 & 2\end{array}\right|$ and $B=\left|\begin{array}{cc}2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right|$, then $A B$ and $B A$ are defined and equal.
en.explanation:
Since, AB is defined.

$\therefore \quad A B=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}2 & 3 & -1 \\ 1 & 4 & 2\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1\end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll}14 & 20 \\ 22 & 25\end{array}\right]$

Also, BA is defined,

$$\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & B A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 \\ 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 3 & -1 \\ 1 & 4 & 2 \end{array}\right] \\ & =\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & 18 & 4 \\ 13 & 32 & 6 \\ 5 & 10 & 0 \end{array}\right] \\ \therefore \quad A B \neq B A \end{array}$$

en.content: If $A$ is skew-symmetric matrix, then $A^2$ is a symmetric matrix.
en.explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \because\quad {\left[A^2\right]^{\prime} } & =\left[A^{\prime}\right]^2 \\ & =[-A]^2 \quad \left[\because A^{\prime}=-A\right]\\ & =A^2 \end{aligned}$$

Hence, A$^2$ is symmetric matrix.

en.content: $(A B)^{-1}=A^{-1} \cdot B^{-1}$, where $A$ and $B$ are invertible matrices satisfying commutative property with respect to multiplication.
en.explanation:
We know that, if $A$ and $B$ are invertible matrices of the same order, then

$$\begin{aligned} (A B)^{-1} & =(B A)^{-1} \quad [\because A B=B A]\\ \text{Here,}\quad (A B)^{-1} & =(A B)^{-1} \\ \Rightarrow\quad B^{-1} A^{-1} & =A^{-1} B^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

[since, $A$ and $B$ are satisfying commutative property with respect to multiplications].
